name: Tiered data acquisition and escalation protocol
consequences: This lever reduces baseline burden and concentrates PSG resources on escalation nights, improving safety and data quality when events are detected. Downstream, it increases data integration complexity and requires robust synchronization and QA to avoid gaps between tiers.
options: Apply nightly low-burden monitoring for all participants while reserving full PSG for escalation nights triggered by sensor flags.
Schedule randomized enhanced nights across participants to build broad PSG datasets without systematic bias.
Define escalation criteria based on multi-sensor anomaly thresholds and implement adaptive full PSG only when those thresholds are met.
review: This lever balances data richness and participant safety, but the options miss contingencies for unanticipated low event yield during baseline nights and potential data gaps between tiers.

name: Expanded recruitment and retention strategy within safety envelope
consequences: Broadening recruitment accelerates enrollment and improves sample diversity while demanding more screening resources and tougher pre-admission safeguards to protect participant safety. It may increase costs and extend timelines if screening bottlenecks arise.
options: Scale recruitment to Bonn and regional clinics while maintaining rigorous pre-admission screening.
Offer flexible stay scheduling and enhanced participant engagement programs to improve retention across multi-week stays.
Establish partnerships with patient networks to streamline referrals and reduce no-show risk.
review: Core tension between recruitment pace and safety screening, but options miss a plan to handle screening bottlenecks and consent fatigue or retention drop-off.

name: Data governance, privacy, and ethical compliance posture
consequences: Clarifies access, storage, and de-identification standards, reducing participant risk and institutional exposure. Downstream, it may constrain data sharing pace and limit some research dissemination, requiring careful balance.
options: Institute de-identification protocols and strict access controls for all data streams.
Launch a controlled-access data portal with formal data-use agreements for external researchers.
Incorporate privacy-by-design reviews into protocol amendments to anticipate evolving risks.
review: Tension between data openness and privacy; options miss explicit procedures for breach response and cross-institution data sharing acceleration.

name: Semi-automated event triage and scoring workflow
consequences: Develops algorithmic ranking of parasomnia events to reduce manual review workload while preserving accuracy. Downstream, requires robust validation, monitoring of false positives, and continued human oversight to prevent drift.
options: Develop a heuristic triage tool that flags likely events for rapid human review.
Train a dual-scorer guided model using existing human scores and refine through cross-validation.
Pilot multi-sensor ensemble methods to improve event specificity before full deployment.
review: Tension between automation efficiency and diagnostic accuracy; options miss a robust, pre-registered validation plan comparing automation to human scoring.

name: Operational safety and staffing model for night coverage
consequences: Ensures safe coverage of multiple suites with a margin for incident response. Downstream, may constrain expansion or increase costs if demand fluctuates or recruitment lags.
options: Maintain a rotating night-shift roster with defined coverage margins and escalation triggers.
Cross-train staff to multiple roles to maximize flexibility during varying event loads.
Institute standardized incident response drills to reduce reaction times on high-risk nights.
review: Tension between safety margins and cost; options miss a strategy to optimize staffing with dynamic demand forecasting.

name: Governance, funding strategy, and external partnerships
consequences: Diversifies funding and oversight, increasing resilience, and decision speed. Downstream, requires more formal reporting and alignment constraints with industry partners.
options: Adopt milestone-based funding releases aligned to enrollment and data quality milestones.
Negotiate time-bound industry partnerships with clear publication governance that preserves academic authorship.
Establish an external scientific advisory board with defined decision rights and triggers for program pivots.
review: Tension between funding stability and governance flexibility; options miss a contingency plan for funding shifts and a formal risk register.

name: Independent external data auditing cadence and ethics oversight
consequences: Establishes an external audit cadence and ethics governance, which enhances data integrity and participant privacy assurance. It introduces independent validation of sensor streams, annotations, and data handling, but creates coordination overhead that can slow internal decision cycles and data access approvals. Downstream, this can extend timelines for data releases and necessitate clear budgeting for governance activities.
options: Institute a standing external ethics board to review participant safety and protocol compliance on a quarterly basis, with published recommendations.
Arrange biannual independent data integrity audits of sensor streams and annotation pipelines to validate scoring reliability.
Formalize a transparent data access policy that requires external sign-off for critical analyses and data releases prior to publication.
review: This lever governs the tension between independent oversight and operational agility, but coordination costs and potential delays are not addressed.

name: Adaptive consenting workflow during prolonged stays
consequences: Enhances participant autonomy and consent validity across extended stays, reducing withdrawal risk and ethical concerns. It also adds administrative workload and potential delays in data access approvals, which may slow recruitment momentum and rise staffing needs. The trade-off is balancing robust consent with practical throughput in a multi-week monitoring environment.
options: Implement staggered re-consent prompts synchronized with stay milestones to refresh scope and data uses.
Provide opt-out pathways for specific data streams while maintaining core participation to sustain dataset.
Automate documentation of consent changes and provide participants with clarity on their rights and data ownership.
review: This lever tackles consent fatigue but may complicate data governance and increase documentation burden.

name: Adaptive multi-modal event annotation workflow
consequences: Improves data fidelity by prioritizing resource allocation on ambiguous or clinically relevant nights, reducing mislabeling and reviewer fatigue. It may require ongoing calibration, retraining, and sufficient staffing to handle escalations, which could create scheduling complexity and potential backlogs if throughput ceilings are reached. The net effect is higher-quality phenotyping with a possible increase in operational friction.
options: Institute phased annotation protocols that escalate review priority when dry-electrode data shows atypical patterns and routine nights are inconclusive.
Adopt dynamic scoring rubrics that adapt to individual participant morphology while preserving inter-rater reliability through periodic calibration sessions.
Pilot automated pre-screening algorithms to triage events and route only probable episodes to human scorers.
review: This lever improves data fidelity but depends on consistent scorer calibration; gaps may still yield drift in labeling.

name: Residential environment safety and wellbeing optimization through behavior-informed design adjustments
consequences: Aligns the on-site environment with naturalistic behavior while maintaining safety margins, which can improve participant comfort and engagement and reduce stress-related artifacts. However, implementing flexible layouts and responsive controls can increase maintenance overhead and create variability across suites, potentially complicating cross-participant comparisons. The downstream effect is enhanced ecological validity with a manageable risk profile.
options: Install sleep-safety features with modular room layouts that can be reconfigured to reduce wandering without compromising domestic feel.
Schedule regular environmental audits with staff and participants to adjust noise, lighting, and privacy settings in response to feedback.
Create a protocol for rapid reconfiguration of suites during escalation nights to maintain safety while preserving naturalistic conditions.
review: This lever aligns environment with natural behavior, but it risks inconsistent safety margins across rooms and times.

name: External collaboration for cross-validation with Bonn Epileptology and sleep research centers
consequences: Expands external validation and triangulation of parasomnia classifications, leveraging broader expertise and datasets to strengthen phenotyping credibility. It can introduce governance and data-sharing friction, require harmonization of protocols, and potentially slow some analyses; however, it also increases external accountability and resilience against single-site bias. The net is deeper science with mitigated risk of over-interpretation.
options: Establish regular joint analysis workshops with epilepsy specialists to cross-validate parasomnia event classifications.
Form data-sharing pilot with regional sleep centers to compare annotation reliability across sites.
Coordinate shared access to anonymized datasets for independent replication of phenotyping results.
review: This lever expands external validation, but alignment of protocols and consent across institutions may introduce governance friction.

name: Phased expansion governance tied to data quality metrics
consequences: Directs growth to match demonstrated data quality, ensuring safety, reliability, and annotation throughput before scaling. It constrains capacity expansion during early phases, preserving focus on core aims, but may slow speed to scale if quality gates prove strict. The trade-off is disciplined growth at the potential cost of opportunity if milestones stall.
options: Define objective data quality gates for expansion that trigger incremental capacity increases only after meeting predefined annotation throughput and reliability.
Publish monthly dashboards that track false alarm rates, inter-rater reliability, and data retention compliance to inform governance decisions.
Institute a staged expansion plan with external review at 6-month intervals to validate alignment with safety and ethical standards.
review: This lever ensures disciplined growth but may slow scaling if quality metrics prove challenging; ensures accountability.

name: Residential environment safety and privacy balance for naturalistic observation
consequences: This lever tightens privacy controls and mitigates participant concern, enabling ethically compliant naturalistic observation in a domestic setting. It reduces exposure-related distress and legal risk while potentially limiting the richness of video data; teams then rely more on non-visual sensors and structured logs, which can affect event detectability and require additional analytics to compensate.
options: Implement privacy-preserving camera placements and access controls to ensure only essential staff view recordings while maintaining clinical observation.
Redesign data collection to emphasize non-visual sensors and encrypted storage, preserving analytical richness while reducing privacy exposure.
Pilot a consent-driven opt-in window for recording during high-risk nights with enhanced observation requirements.
review: This lever balances privacy with ecological validity, but the options overlook the practical costs and operational overhead of implementing privacy-preserving infrastructure.

name: Robust night-shift staffing with cross-coverage and rapid-response protocols
consequences: This lever increases safety and reduces missed events during overnight monitoring; downstream: higher labor costs and scheduling complexity, with potential fatigue risk if night shifts extend too long.
options: Cross-train staff across sleep monitoring, patient safety, and rapid-response signaling to ensure continuous coverage during overnight shifts and rapid escalation.
Institute staggered shift blocks with built-in floaters to guarantee minimum team coverage during peak event windows.
Conduct regular nocturnal safety drills and post-event debriefs to sharpen decision-making under pressure.
review: This lever resolves staffing resilience versus cost, but options miss burnout risk and long-term staff retention dynamics.

name: Phased data capture cadence alignment with clinical escalation nights
consequences: Aligning data capture cadence improves data yield on escalation nights and avoids unnecessary full PSG on every night; downstream: scheduling overhead and potential bottlenecks if escalation nights are mismatched with participant stay length.
options: Synchronize tiered monitoring with escalation-night windows to maximize high-resolution data without imposing unnecessary workload.
Allocate dedicated PSG calibration nights to validate sensor fusion and improve detection fidelity.
Implement automated scheduling rules that trigger escalation PSG when sensor anomalies indicate potential parasomnia events.
review: This lever addresses scheduling efficiency, but options miss potential data management bottlenecks during escalation nights.

name: Structured inter-rater calibration and scoring coherence program
consequences: Enhances consistency between two independent raters and reduces variability in annotation; downstream: demands ongoing training time and can create perceived audit pressure.
options: Mandate weekly cross-review sessions where two experienced raters reconcile discrepancies on a representative annotation set.
Institute a standardized scoring rubric and quarterly recalibration to stabilize inter-rater reliability.
Incorporate randomized audits of a sample of annotations to detect drift and bias and correct course.
review: This lever strengthens scoring reliability, but options miss the risk of over-standardization reducing nuance in atypical events.

name: Regional external validation partnerships for parasomnia event annotation
consequences: Enhances external credibility and cross-site validation; downstream: coordination overhead and potential delays due to partner schedules or differing regulatory constraints.
options: Formalize partnerships with Bonn-area sleep centers to cross-validate event annotations on shared de-identified datasets.
Establish joint workshops with partner centers to align scoring criteria and harmonize nomenclature across sites.
Create a data-sharing memorandum of understanding and periodic benchmarking with external labs for independent scoring validation.
review: This lever opens external validation, but options miss the risk of data-sharing delays and regulatory alignment across sites.

name: Adaptive admission pathways and consent-based scheduling to improve enrollment stability
consequences: Improves enrollment stability and study throughput; downstream: introduces consent-management complexity and potential cohort heterogeneity that can complicate downstream analyses.
options: Offer flexible admission windows and consent packages to accommodate participant preferences while maintaining safety and study integrity.
Implement an enhanced pre-screening portal to streamline eligibility checks and reduce late-screening dropouts.
Pilot rolling enrollment cohorts that maintain recruitment momentum while preserving consistent study timelines.
review: This lever improves enrollment tempo, but options miss potential ethical complexity around consent variations across cohorts.
